request: We'd like managers to edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed.

problem.value: Managers need a way to correct/update signed documents without losing legal validity — likely errors found post-signature or terms that changed.
problem.evidence: Client only states the desired capability ('edit and re-approve'), not the underlying trigger.

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — unclear if today they re-sign a new doc manually, ignore the issue, or handle it outside the platform.

actors.value: Managers trigger edit + re-approval. Unclear who else is involved: original signatories, records/compliance officer.

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval/Signatory — standard platform entities, edit implies a new Version.
business_objects.evidence: Domain notes: Document/Version split is core to this platform.

business_rules.value: Unclear which managers, under which conditions (any content change vs. correction only), and whether it applies to all classifications/document types or only some.
business_rules.evidence: Request gives no conditions or scope for the edit right.

workflow.value: Likely maps to the platform's supersession pattern: editing a signed doc creates a new version, reopens signature/approval, old version retained as superseded — not an in-place edit.
workflow.evidence: Context: 'edit the document' almost always means 'supersede it with a new version'; signed content is frozen.

integrations.value: Unclear who/what must be notified when a signed doc is re-approved (prior signatories, downstream systems referencing the old version).

permissions.value: Likely restricted to managers with an owner/approver role on that document type; must respect classification-based access.
permissions.evidence: Context: access and edit rights on approved docs are sensitive and cross-cutting.

config_vs_custom.value: Re-approval circuit for edited documents is probably client-configurable, like original approval circuits.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Platform config notes: approval circuits are client-specific.

constraints.value: Electronic-signature validity and audit-trail obligations likely constrain how re-signature is handled.
constraints.evidence: Context: 'electronic-signature validity' and audit trail listed as regulatory sensitivities.

edge_cases.value: What happens to references/links pointing to the old signed version? Concurrent edits by two managers? Partial re-approval (one signatory approves, another rejects)?
edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed; matches platform's known trap on supersession.

reporting.value: Must log who edited what, previous vs. new version, re-approval trail — audit trail is a hard requirement for this platform.
reporting.evidence: Context: audit trail 'must never have gaps'; compliance officer role exists precisely for this.

risks.value: Risk of managers overusing edit-and-reapprove to bypass proper amendment control, or old superseded versions being mistakenly treated as current.
risks.evidence: Context: 'approved ≠ published/current' confusion is a classic trap.

questions.q: When a manager 'edits' a signed document, should this create a new version (old one retained as superseded, signatures reset) or do you expect the existing signed content to be changed directly?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: this is the platform's core trap — in-place edit vs. supersession are fundamentally different builds affecting data model, legal validity and audit trail.

questions.q: Which managers can trigger this — any manager, only the original approver/document owner, or one scoped by document classification/type? Under what conditions (correction of an error vs. any content change)?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: defines the access rule and prevents an overly broad edit right on legally-signed content.

questions.q: Does re-approval need to go through the same signatories/circuit as the original signature, or can it be a different/lighter circuit?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: determines whether we reuse the existing approval engine or need a distinct re-approval path.

questions.q: What must happen to the old signed version and anything referencing it (links, citations, downstream systems) once the new version is approved?
questions.why: medium-high impact × high uncertainty: unresolved, this is where 'wrong version acted upon' errors occur.

questions.q: Do you need a full audit trail (who edited, why, old vs new value, re-approval history) visible to compliance/audit, and is this a regulatory requirement in your context?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: an audit trail is a structural requirement on this platform, not a bolt-on — confirming scope now avoids costly retrofits.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify a document that has already been signed and have it re-approved, without breaking the legal validity or traceability of the original signature.
summary.scope: Likely implementation of a supersession flow for signed documents: editing creates a new version, triggers a new approval/signature circuit, retains the old version as superseded, and reconciles anything pointing to it — layered on top of the existing Document/Version and approval-circuit infrastructure.
assumptions: Editing a signed document means creating a new version (supersession), not mutating the signed content in place.
The re-approval circuit reuses the existing approval/signature engine, possibly with the same signatories.
Only managers with ownership/approval rights on the document (respecting its classification) can trigger this.
A full audit trail (who edited, previous vs new version, re-approval history) is required given the platform's compliance context.
Editing applies at the document-type/classification level, not universally to all documents.
summary.blind_spot: The request reads as a simple 'allow edit + re-approve' toggle, but on this platform a signed document's content is frozen — 'editing' it almost always means creating a new version that reopens signature, invalidates or re-collects prior approvals, and leaves the old version retained as superseded. Building this as an in-place edit would silently break signature validity and the audit trail; the client has not indicated which of these two very different builds they expect.

problem.value: Managers need a controlled way to correct/update documents that have already been signed, without losing the validity and traceability of the original signature.
problem.evidence: 'edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed'

current_process.evidence: not stated — unknown whether managers currently cannot edit at all, or re-upload/re-issue manually outside the system

actors.value: Managers edit and re-approve; original signatories/approvers and other stakeholders referencing the old version are likely also involved but unnamed.

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval/Signatory — a signed document is a frozen Version; editing implies a new Version tied to the same Document identity.
business_objects.evidence: context card: Document vs Version pattern

business_rules.value: Editing a signed document should trigger supersession (new version) rather than in-place mutation; scope of which document types/classifications this applies to is undefined.
business_rules.evidence: context: 'edit a signed/approved document hides supersession'; request only says managers can edit+re-approve, no conditions given

workflow.value: Likely: manager edits -> new draft version created -> re-approval circuit runs -> on approval, new version becomes current/published, old one marked superseded. Exact state transitions and interim status of the old version are unconfirmed.
workflow.evidence: context: two independent lifecycles (approval vs publication) and supersession pattern

integrations.evidence: not stated — no info on who/what must be notified when a signed doc is superseded

permissions.value: Managers can edit; unclear if the editing manager can also be the re-approver (segregation-of-duties risk) or if re-approval must go to a distinct approver/original circuit.
permissions.evidence: request names only 'managers' for both edit and re-approve, which is itself the ambiguity

config_vs_custom.evidence: not stated — platform is multi-client configurable; approval circuits and classification rules are normally client-specific per context card

constraints.value: Legal validity of the original e-signature must not be silently broken; audit trail obligations apply since signed/approved documents are involved.

edge_cases.evidence: not stated — behavior of in-flight references to the old signed version during re-approval window is undefined

reporting.value: An audit trail (who edited, why, previous vs new version, re-approval history) is required given the signed/regulatory nature of the documents — not optional.
reporting.evidence: context signal: audit trail is a hard requirement for compliance on signed/approved documents

risks.value: Risk of breaking signature validity, of ambiguous 'current' document (approved vs published), and of stale references (links, citations, downstream exports) pointing at the superseded version.
risks.evidence: context: 'approved != published/current', 'anything pointing at the old version reconciled'

questions.q: When a manager edits a signed document, should this always create a new version that goes through re-approval (supersession), or is there a case where the signed content itself is changed in place?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred, 15% complete) x high impact: this decides between a versioned-supersession build and an in-place edit that breaks signature validity — the two are fundamentally different systems.

questions.q: Can the same manager who edits the document also be the one who re-approves it, or must re-approval go through a distinct approver / the original signatory circuit (segregation of duties)?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred, 20% complete) x high impact: the request names only 'managers' for both steps, which is a compliance-relevant ambiguity — wrong assumption here means rebuilding the approval circuit.

questions.q: While the new version is being edited and re-approved, is the previously signed version still considered valid/current, or is it immediately frozen/hidden pending the new approval?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred, 20% complete) x high impact: conflates the approval and publication lifecycles — determines what happens to in-flight references and what users see during the re-approval window.

questions.q: Does this apply to all document types/classifications uniformly, or only specific ones (e.g. contracts vs internal policies), and does the re-approval circuit differ by client or document type?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: on a multi-client configurable platform, approval circuits are client-specific — this changes whether the solution is a generic rule or per-client configuration.

questions.q: What currently happens today when a manager needs to correct a signed document — is it forbidden, done manually outside the system, or something else?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: reveals the real pain point and whether this is a net-new capability or formalizing an existing workaround, which changes scope and urgency.

summary.objective: Allow managers to correct an already-signed document and have the correction go through re-approval, while preserving the legal validity and auditability of the original signature.
summary.scope: Likely implementation: editing a signed document creates a new draft Version (supersession) rather than mutating the signed one; the new version runs through a re-approval circuit; on approval it becomes the current version while the prior signed version is retained and marked superseded, with a full audit trail of who changed what and why.
assumptions: Editing triggers a new version rather than in-place modification of signed content
Manager and re-approver may need to be different people (segregation of duties), though not stated
Standard approval/publication lifecycle pattern applies (approved != published/current)
An audit trail of edits and re-approvals is required for compliance
Prior signed version remains referenceable after supersession
summary.blind_spot: The request says 'edit and re-approve' as if it were a single simple action, but it actually hides: (1) whether this is an in-place edit or a full supersession with a new version, (2) who is allowed to re-approve versus who edited, and (3) what status the original signed document holds while the correction is pending — get any of these wrong and the fix either breaks signature validity or creates an audit gap.

problem.value: Likely need to correct or update content in already-signed documents (error fix, contract change, policy update) without losing the compliance trail.
problem.evidence: Request focuses on the mechanic ('edit and re-approve') not the underlying need.

current_process.evidence: Not stated whether signed docs are currently immutable or handled via manual workaround (new doc, side letter, email).

actors.value: Managers are the actor who edits and re-approves; unclear if they are also original signatories/approvers or a distinct role.

business_objects.value: Document, Version, Approval, Signatory — the edit almost certainly must produce a new Version rather than mutate the signed one.
business_objects.evidence: Context: 'a later edit is a new version, never an in-place change.'

business_rules.value: Unclear whether re-approval requires the full original circuit (quorum, same approvers) or can be a lighter manager-only sign-off, and what happens to prior signatures.
business_rules.evidence: Request implies a shortcut ('manager edits and re-approves') that may bypass the original approval circuit.

workflow.value: Editing a signed doc should trigger supersession: new draft version, prior version frozen/retained, re-approval re-opens the approval lifecycle; publication status must be handled separately (was it published?).
workflow.evidence: Context: 'edit the document' almost always means 'supersede it with a new version' — different build from patching a field.

integrations.value: Who/what must be notified when a signed document is edited and re-approved (original signatories, readers of the published version, downstream systems referencing the old version)?

permissions.value: Likely only managers (not any author) can initiate edit-after-signature; scope (which document types/classifications) undefined.
permissions.evidence: 'managers' named as sole actor, but no rule on which documents/classifications this applies to.

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear if this is a global platform behavior or a client-specific override of the approval circuit.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: approval circuits and classification schemes are client-specific configuration.

constraints.value: Electronic-signature validity and audit trail requirements likely apply — re-approving a previously signed document touches legal validity of signatures.
constraints.evidence: Context: 'electronic-signature validity', 'audit trail is a hard requirement.'

edge_cases.value: What if the document was already published/distributed? Under legal hold? Mid-approval when edit is requested? Multiple pending edits at once?
edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed; context flags supersession, legal hold, and approved-vs-published as recurring traps.

reporting.value: An audit trail showing old vs new version, who edited, who re-approved, and when, is almost certainly mandatory given the compliance context.
reporting.evidence: Context: 'an audit trail is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have — retrofitting it late is expensive.'

risks.value: Risk of confusing 'edited/re-approved' with 'still the current/published version' if publication isn't updated in lockstep.
risks.evidence: Context: 'approved ≠ published/current' is a classic confusion source.

questions.q: When a manager 'edits' a signed document, should this create a new version (old one retained, frozen, and referenceable) while the prior signatures are invalidated or re-collected — or did you picture something closer to an in-place correction?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, inferred) x impact(high): this decides whether we build supersession + versioning or a much cheaper (and non-compliant) patch — the single biggest cost driver here.

questions.q: Does re-approval need to go through the same approval circuit/quorum as the original signature, or can the manager alone re-approve? What happens to the original signatories' signatures — void, or must they re-sign too?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) x impact(high): changes whether this is a manager-only shortcut or a full re-run of a multi-step circuit — directly affects legal validity of the result.

questions.q: If the document was already published/distributed, does editing it also require re-publication, or should the old published version stay 'current' until the new one is separately published?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) x impact(high): approval and publication are two independent lifecycles in this system — conflating them is the classic trap and changes who sees what, when.

questions.q: Is 'managers can edit and re-approve signed documents' a global rule for all documents, or specific to certain document types, classification levels, or clients?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) x impact(high): approval circuits and classification schemes are client-specific configuration — a global rule vs. a scoped one are very different builds.

questions.q: Who needs to be notified when a signed document is edited and re-approved (original signatories, current readers, downstream systems referencing the old version), and do we need a visible audit trail of old-vs-new content and approvals?
questions.why: uncertainty(medium, inferred) x impact(high): compliance context makes the audit trail mandatory, not optional — worth confirming scope now rather than retrofitting.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify the content of a document that has already been signed/approved, and have it re-approved, while preserving the integrity of the prior signed record.
summary.scope: Likely requires: a new Version created on edit (supersession) rather than in-place mutation; the prior signed version frozen and retained; a re-approval step that re-opens the approval/signature lifecycle (scope and rigor TBD); handling of the separate publication/distribution lifecycle so 'current' vs 'approved' isn't confused; an audit trail of who edited/re-approved what and when; permission rules scoping which managers can do this on which documents.
assumptions: Editing a signed document should produce a new version, not mutate the signed one, mirroring the platform's standard supersession pattern.
Managers are a distinct, elevated role allowed to initiate this, separate from original document authors.
An audit trail of the edit/re-approval is required given the compliance sensitivity of signed documents.
Original signatures are invalidated or must be re-collected when a re-approval occurs (not simply left standing).
summary.blind_spot: The request conflates 'signed/approved' with 'published/current'. If the document was already published, editing and re-approving it does not automatically make the new version the one people see and act on — publication is a separate lifecycle. Without an explicit decision, the platform could end up with a re-approved version that readers never actually receive, or worse, a published superseded version that people keep treating as current.

briefs.problem: Signed documents sometimes need correction or updating, but nothing today lets that happen without breaking legal validity or the audit trail.
briefs.solution: An edit-and-re-approve capability for signed documents, built on the platform's existing version/supersession pattern rather than in-place editing.
introduces: a supersession-on-signed-document engine (new version, old retained, signatures reset)
a re-approval/re-signature workflow distinct from — or reusing — the original signing circuit
reference reconciliation for anything pointing at the now-superseded version
an extended audit trail capturing edit reason, old-vs-new diff and re-approval history
classification-aware permission scoping for who may trigger an edit on signed content
notification routing to prior signatories and downstream consumers of the old version

challenges.headline: "Edit and re-approve" reads as in-place edit
challenges.premise: The request's own language — edit, then re-approve — suggests the signed content itself gets changed and then re-signed.
challenges.alternative: This platform's established pattern is supersession: a new version is created, the old one is frozen and retained, and only the new version goes through signature again.
challenges.consequence: If built literally as an in-place edit, it silently invalidates the original signature's legal standing and leaves a gap in the audit trail the moment content changes after signing.
challenges.recommendation: Confirm explicitly with the client that this is a new-version/supersession flow, not a mutation of signed content, before any design work starts.

challenges.headline: Full re-signature circuit for every edit
challenges.premise: The model assumes re-approval reuses the same signatories/circuit as the original signature, for any edit.
challenges.alternative: Many document platforms distinguish a minor correction (typo, formatting) from a substantive change, and only require full re-signature for the latter — a lighter review for the former.
challenges.consequence: Forcing every correction through the full original circuit makes the feature heavy enough that managers may bypass it and fix documents outside the platform, undermining the audit trail it's meant to protect.
challenges.recommendation: Ask the client whether they distinguish correction-type edits from substantive changes, and whether the re-approval circuit should be configurable per document type or classification.

challenges.headline: Unscoped manager edit right
challenges.premise: The request implies any manager can edit and re-approve a signed document.
challenges.alternative: Access to signed, potentially confidential or restricted documents is normally scoped to the document owner or original approver, and gated by the document's classification, not by role alone.
challenges.consequence: A broad, unscoped edit right on legally signed content is a governance exposure — it invites bypassing formal amendment control and risks a classification breach.
challenges.recommendation: Define precisely which managers qualify (ownership, classification, document type) before scoping permissions, reusing the existing classification-based access model.

complexity_reasons: must reconcile two independent lifecycles (approval and publication) under a supersession event
reference reconciliation for links/citations pointing at the old version
audit trail must capture full diff and re-approval history with no gaps
re-approval circuit likely configurable per client/classification, not a single fixed path
briefs.cost_driver: the supersession-and-reconciliation logic: creating the new version, resetting/re-collecting signatures, and keeping every reference and audit record consistent with which version is current
risks: The existing approval-circuit engine may not natively support 'reopen and re-collect' semantics for an already-signed document — likely needs extension, not reuse as-is
No edge cases defined yet for concurrent edits by two managers or partial re-approval (one signatory approves, one rejects)
Classification-based access could be bypassed if the edit-trigger permission isn't wired into the same access-control layer as read/download
Retrofitting the audit trail after build is expensive on this platform — it's flagged as a hard requirement, not a later add-on
Legal exposure if old signatures aren't properly invalidated/marked superseded rather than just hidden

opportunities.text: Generalize the supersession-on-signed-content pattern (new version, old retained, reopens signature) as a reusable primitive — the platform already applies the identical logic to contract amendments.
Build the re-approval circuit as a configurable variant of the existing approval-circuit engine rather than a parallel workflow.
Extend the audit-trail/diff mechanism (who changed what, previous vs new value) as a shared service usable wherever a 'frozen then re-opened' pattern exists.

next_steps: Get explicit client confirmation that 'edit' means creating a new superseding version, not mutating signed content — this decides the entire architecture.
Define precisely who can trigger an edit on a signed document (ownership, role, classification) before touching permissions.
Clarify whether re-approval always requires the full original signatory circuit or whether a lighter path exists for minor corrections.
Nail down audit-trail and reporting expectations now, given this platform's compliance obligations — retrofitting later is costly.

decisions.decision: Editing a signed document creates a new version (supersession) rather than mutating the signed content in place
decisions.why: The platform's frozen-after-approval rule and legal-signature validity require the prior signed content to remain intact and referenceable.
decisions.alternative: Mutating the signed content directly and re-running approval on the same document record.
decisions.tradeoff: Slightly more storage and reference-management complexity, in exchange for preserving legal validity and a clean audit trail.

decisions.decision: The old version is retained and stamped superseded rather than deleted or hidden

open_decisions: Which managers/roles are permitted to trigger an edit on a signed document, and does this vary by document type or classification?
Does re-approval always require the full original signatory circuit, or can a lighter path apply to minor corrections?
What happens to existing references/links pointing at the superseded version — auto-redirect, flag, or leave for manual reconciliation?
What are the concrete success metrics for this feature (fewer manual workarounds, audit findings closed, etc.)?

briefs.problem: Managers have no controlled way to correct a signed/approved document once frozen, so corrections likely happen off-system or by unsafe in-place edits that quietly break signature validity and the audit trail.
briefs.solution: A supersession workflow: editing a signed document creates a new version that runs through re-approval, while the original stays intact, retained, and marked superseded.
introduces: a version-supersession engine tied to a stable Document identity
a re-approval circuit distinct from (or reusing) the original signing circuit
explicit handling of two lifecycles at once — approval validity vs. what's currently published
an audit trail surface capturing who edited, why, and the full version/approval history
reconciliation logic for anything referencing the old version (links, exports, downstream modules)

challenges.headline: Editor also acting as re-approver
challenges.premise: The request names only 'managers' for both editing and re-approving, implying the same person can do both.
challenges.alternative: Standard segregation-of-duties practice routes re-approval to a distinct approver, or replays the original signatory circuit.
challenges.consequence: A manager who edits and self-approves undermines the legal and audit value of re-approval entirely — the correction has no independent check, which is exactly what auditors and e-signature validity rules look for.
challenges.recommendation: Confirm with compliance/legal whether the editing manager can be in the re-approval chain before building permissions.

challenges.headline: 'Edit' implies in-place change
challenges.premise: The phrase 'edit and re-approve' reads as directly modifying the signed document.
challenges.alternative: The platform's own standard is that an approved version is frozen — a correction is a new version superseding the old one, never a field patch.
challenges.consequence: Building this as an in-place edit invalidates the original signature and creates an unreconcilable audit trail — it's not a smaller version of this feature, it's a different and non-compliant one.
challenges.recommendation: State explicitly to the client that this ships as version supersession, not document editing, before estimating.

challenges.headline: No defined status during re-approval window
challenges.premise: The request assumes editing and re-approving is effectively instantaneous, with no thought to what's valid in between.
challenges.alternative: Keep the original signed version live/published as 'current' until the new version clears re-approval, only swapping over on approval.
challenges.consequence: Without this, there's a window where the document is either invisible, shown as draft to people who shouldn't see it, or ambiguously 'current' — exactly the approved-vs-published confusion this platform is built to avoid.
challenges.recommendation: Define an explicit interim state (e.g. 'under revision, prior version still current') before building the workflow.

complexity_reasons: must track approval and publication lifecycles separately without conflating them
supersession/versioning logic tied to a persistent Document identity
segregation-of-duties enforcement layered onto existing permission rules
audit trail must capture full diff and re-approval history for compliance from day one
briefs.cost_driver: the supersession + re-approval engine, including how it interacts with the existing approval circuit
risks: The existing approval-circuit framework may only support first-time signing, not a distinct 're-approval after correction' path — likely needs extension
Other modules or documents referencing the old signed version (citations, exports, downstream approvals) may silently point at stale data after supersession
If built as in-place editing rather than versioning, the original e-signature's legal validity is compromised
Retrofitting the audit trail later is expensive; it needs to be designed in from the start given the compliance stakes here

opportunities.text: Generalize the version-supersession pattern (frozen version, new version on edit, prior retained) as a shared engine rather than a one-off for this feature.
The 'edit triggers a new cycle, original stays valid until superseded' pattern mirrors the Contract/Amendment lifecycle — worth aligning the two so future rule changes aren't duplicated.
A distinct re-approval circuit (separate from first-signature circuit) could be reused for any future 'correction after finalization' workflow.

next_steps: Confirm with the client that this is a version-supersession build, not an in-place edit, before estimating.
Settle segregation-of-duties rules: can the editing manager also re-approve, or must it route elsewhere?
Define the interim state and visibility of the original signed version during the re-approval window.
Clarify whether this applies uniformly across all document types/classifications or is scoped to specific ones, and whether the circuit varies by client.

decisions.decision: Editing a signed document produces a new version rather than mutating the signed content
decisions.why: The platform's frozen-after-approval rule is standard across all clients and existing document types.
decisions.alternative: In-place modification of the signed content.
decisions.tradeoff: Added versioning and reconciliation complexity, in exchange for preserving signature validity and audit integrity.

decisions.decision: A full audit trail of edits and re-approvals (who, what, when, previous vs. new version) is mandatory

open_decisions: Whether the editing manager can also be the re-approver, or approval must go to a distinct party
What status the original signed version holds while the new version is under re-approval
Whether this applies to all document types/classifications or a defined subset
Whether the re-approval circuit is uniform or configurable per client
What the current workaround is today, to size this as new capability vs. formalizing an existing manual process

briefs.problem: Managers have no compliant way to correct or update a document after it's been signed without breaking the audit trail or the validity of prior signatures.
briefs.solution: A supersession-based edit workflow: editing a signed document spins up a new version, re-runs approval, and keeps publication status in sync, instead of patching the signed record in place.
introduces: a version/supersession engine layered on the existing approval circuit
a re-approval sub-workflow that re-opens a previously closed signature lifecycle
explicit handling of publication status when an approved-but-published document is superseded
an audit trail spanning edit, re-approval and republication events
permission scoping by document type and classification for who may trigger an edit-after-signature
notification routing to prior signatories and current readers of the superseded version

challenges.headline: Manager-only re-approval shortcut
challenges.premise: The request frames this as managers editing and re-approving, implying the manager alone can validate the new version.
challenges.alternative: Document-management platforms typically require the new version to pass through the same circuit (quorum, same approvers, or a defined lighter path) precisely because the original approval carried legal weight.
challenges.consequence: If a manager can unilaterally re-approve, one role effectively overrides a multi-party sign-off, which can invalidate the document's legal standing and defeat the purpose of the original circuit.
challenges.recommendation: Confirm with legal or compliance whether re-approval must replicate the original circuit, and under what conditions a lighter path is acceptable.

challenges.headline: Global edit right, not scoped
challenges.premise: The request treats 'managers can edit and re-approve signed documents' as a blanket capability.
challenges.alternative: Approval circuits and classification schemes in this platform are client- and document-type-specific; the right to reopen a signed document likely needs the same scoping, such as restricted document types or confidentiality levels.
challenges.consequence: An unscoped rule risks letting managers reopen documents that should never be editable this way (e.g. under legal hold or restricted classification), forcing a rebuild once real scoping surfaces.
challenges.recommendation: Define which document types, classifications and clients this capability applies to before implementation.

challenges.headline: Re-approved does not mean republished
challenges.premise: The request implicitly assumes that re-approving the edited document makes it the version people now see and act on.
challenges.alternative: Approval and publication are independent lifecycles in this system; a newly re-approved version could sit unpublished while the old superseded version stays 'current' for readers.
challenges.consequence: Without an explicit publish step, the platform could produce a technically-approved new version nobody actually receives, or leave a superseded version visibly current, the exact confusion the platform exists to prevent.
challenges.recommendation: Decide explicitly whether re-approval auto-triggers republication or requires a separate publish action, and surface this clearly to users.

complexity_reasons: versioning and supersession logic layered on an existing approval engine
re-opening a closed signature lifecycle without corrupting prior signatures
two lifecycles (approval and publication) that must stay synchronized but not conflated
audit trail spanning edit, re-approval, and publication events
briefs.cost_driver: the re-approval workflow that must re-open a previously closed signature lifecycle while preserving the original signed version intact
risks: The existing approval-circuit engine was built for a linear draft-to-approved flow; re-triggering it from a signed state is a new transition it may not support cleanly.
Original signatures may need explicit invalidation or re-collection; getting this wrong compromises the platform's e-signature validity claim.
Classification and access rules are cross-cutting; if the new version doesn't correctly inherit or re-evaluate classification, visibility could leak or block incorrectly.
Publication drift: if publish isn't explicitly re-triggered, readers may keep acting on a stale superseded version indefinitely.
Retrofitting the audit trail later rather than building it in from day one is called out as expensive in this domain.

opportunities.text: Formalize the version/supersession pattern as a reusable engine rather than a one-off for this edit case, since editing after freeze is a recurring pattern across the platform.

Build the publication-sync logic (re-approved to republish decision) as a distinct, explicit step rather than folding it into re-approval, so future flows like retention expiry or reclassification can reuse the same publish-decision surface.
Generalize the re-approval sub-workflow so other post-approval mutations, such as reclassification or retention changes, can trigger the same lightweight or full re-approval logic.

next_steps: Confirm whether editing a signed document must run the full original approval circuit or a defined lighter re-approval path, and what happens to prior signatures.
Define the scope of who can trigger this (which managers, on which document types or classifications) before estimating.
Decide explicitly how re-approval interacts with publication status, so approved-but-unpublished and published-but-superseded states are never ambiguous.
Confirm the audit trail requirements (what must be logged, retained, and exportable) with compliance before build, since retrofitting it later is costly.

decisions.decision: Editing a signed document creates a new version rather than mutating the existing one
decisions.why: The platform's standard pattern freezes content after approval; in-place mutation would break the frozen-after-approval guarantee and any prior signatures or citations pointing at that version.
decisions.alternative: In-place correction of the signed document.
decisions.tradeoff: More building (versioning, supersession, re-approval) in exchange for preserving audit integrity and signature validity.

open_decisions: Whether re-approval requires the full original circuit or a lighter manager-only path, and what happens to prior signatures
Which document types, classifications, or clients this edit-after-signature capability applies to
Whether re-approval automatically triggers republication or is a separate action
Who must be notified when a signed document is edited and re-approved
What audit trail detail is required for compliance sign-off
